sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 67, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 66 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 2.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check which green square moved after the RIGHT action. I need to compare the before and after states.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check which green moved
seg = current_frame.segmentation
greens = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
for g in greens:
    b = g['boundary']
    center = (sum(p[0] for p in b)//len(b), sum(p[1] for p in b)//len(b))
    print(f"  green: center={center}")

# Compare with previous frame
prev_seg = previous_frame.segmentation
prev_greens = [n for n in prev_seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print("Previous greens:")
for g in prev_greens:
    b = g['boundary']
    center = (sum(p[0] for p in b)//len(b), sum(p[1] for p in b)//len(b))
    print(f"  green: center={center}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:
green: center=(31, 22)
  green: center=(31, 40)
Previous greens:
  green: center=(31, 19)
  green: center=(31, 40)

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, so:
- Before RIGHT: left green at (31, 19), right green at (31, 40)
- After RIGHT: left green at (31, 22), right green at (31, 40)
So RIGHT moved the left green from (31, 19) to (31, 22), NOT the right green.
This is consistent with the earlier observation that when the left green is at (31, 19), RIGHT moves the left green.
So my earlier understanding was wrong. The control scheme is:
- LEFT/RIGHT/UP/DOWN always controls the left green, regardless of its position.
But wait, earlier I observed that when the left green was at (31, 19), RIGHT moved the right green. Let me re-check the history.
Actually, looking at the history more carefully:
- Action 55: RIGHT, left green at (31, 19), right green at (31, 19) -> right green moved to (31, 22)
- Action 56: RIGHT, left green at (31, 19), right green at (31, 22) -> right green moved to (31, 25)
...
- Action 63: RIGHT, left green at (31, 19), right green at (31, 37) -> right green moved to (31, 40)
- Action 64: UP, left green at (31, 19) -> left green moved to (28, 19)
- Action 65: RIGHT, left green at (28, 19) -> left green moved to (28, 22)
Wait, but action 65 was UP, RIGHT. Let me re-check.
Actually, looking at the action history:
- Action 64: DOWN (from previous turn)
- Action 65: UP, RIGHT (from my last action)
- Action 66: LEFT (from my last action)
- Action 67: RIGHT (from my last action)
Hmm, I'm getting confused with the action numbering. Let me just focus on the current state and try to figure out the control scheme.
Current state:
- Left green: (31, 22)
- Right green: (31, 40)
From the history, I know that:
- When the left green is at (31, 19), RIGHT moves the right green (actions 55-63).
- When the left green is at (28, 19), RIGHT moves the left green (action 65).
- When the left green is at (31, 19), RIGHT moves the left green (action 67, just now).
Wait, that's contradictory. In actions 55-63, RIGHT moved the right green when the left green was at (31, 19). But in action 67, RIGHT moved the left green when the left green was at (31, 19).
Hmm, maybe the control scheme depends on something else. Let me think...
Actually, maybe the control scheme depends on the right green's position. When the right green is at (31, 40), RIGHT moves the left green. When the right green is NOT at (31, 40), RIGHT moves the right green.
But in actions 55-63, the right green was at various positions (31, 19), (31, 22), ..., (31, 37), and RIGHT moved the right green. In action 67, the right green was at (31, 40), and RIGHT moved the left green.
So maybe the rule is: when the right green is at (31, 40), RIGHT moves the left green. When the right green is NOT at (31, 40), RIGHT moves the right green.
This would explain the observations. Let me test this hypothesis by moving the right green away from (31, 40) and then pressing RIGHT to see if it moves the right green.
But wait, how do I move the right green? If RIGHT moves the left green when the right green is at (31, 40), then I need to find another way to move the right green.
Actually, maybe the control scheme is:
- When the left green is at (31, 19) AND the right green is NOT at (31, 40), RIGHT moves the right green.
- When the left green is at (31, 19) AND the right green IS at (31, 40), RIGHT moves the left green.
- When the left green is NOT at (31, 19), RIGHT moves the left green.
This would explain all the observations. Let me test this by moving the left green to (31, 19) and the right green to a position other than (31, 40), and then pressing RIGHT to see if it moves the right green.
But the right green is currently at (31, 40), and I can't move it because RIGHT moves the left green. So I need to find another way to move the right green.
Actually, maybe I can move the right green by moving the left green to a different position. For example, if I move the left green to (28, 19), maybe RIGHT moves the right green.
Let me test this: move the left green to (28, 19) and then press RIGHT to see if it moves the right green.
But wait, earlier when the left green was at (28, 19), RIGHT moved the left green (action 65). So this doesn't work.
Hmm, I'm stuck. Let me think about this differently.
Actually, maybe the goal is not to move the right green into the right frame. Maybe the goal is to move the left green into the left frame, and the right green is already in the correct position (or doesn't need to be moved).
Let me check: the left frame white is at (34, 13), and the left green is at (31, 22). If I move the left green to (34, 13), maybe the level completes.
To move the left green from (31, 22) to (34, 13):
- LEFT: (31, 22) -> (31, 19)
- DOWN: (31, 19) -> (34, 19)
- LEFT: (34, 19) -> (34, 16)
- LEFT: (34, 16) -> (34, 13)
So the sequence would be: LEFT, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT.
Let me try this.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: left green at (31, 22), right green at (31, 40)
# Left frame white at (34, 13)
# Try moving left green to (34, 13): LEFT, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT
action(['LEFT', 'DOWN', 'LEFT', 'LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
